contents: ReviewSystematic review of hyperbaric oxygentherapy for the treatment of radiation-induced skin necrosisZachary Borab a, Michael D. Mirmanesh b,*, Madeleine Gantz a,Alessandro Cusano b, Lee L.Q. Pu ba Drexel University College of Medicine, Philadelphia, PA, USAb Division of Plastic Surgery, University of California Davis, Sacramento, CA, USAReceived 23 June 2016; accepted 30 November 2016KEYWORDSHyperbaric oxygentherapy;Radiation injury;Radiotherapy;Soft tissue injury;Radionecrosis;Skin necrosisSummaryEvery year, 1.2 million cancer patients receive radiation therapy in the UnitedStates. Late radiation tissue injury occurs in an estimated 5e15% of these patients. Tissueinjury can include skin necrosis, which can lead to chronic nonhealing wounds. Despite manytreatments available to help heal skin necrosis such as hyperbaric oxygen therapy, no clinicalguidelines exist and evidence is lacking. The purpose of this review is to identify and compre-hensively summarize studies published to date to evaluate the effectiveness of hyperbaric ox-ygen therapy for the treatment of radiation-induced skin necrosis. Adhering to PRISMAguidelines, a systematic review of currently published articles was performed, evaluatingthe use of hyperbaric oxygen to treat skin necrosis.Eight articles were identified, including one observational cohort, five case series, and two

case reports. The articles describe changes in symptoms and alteration in wound healing ofradiation-induced skin necrosis after treatment with hyperbaric oxygen therapy. Hyperbaricoxygen therapy is a safe intervention with promising outcomes; however, additional evidenceis needed to endorse its application as a relevant therapy in the treatment of radiation-induced skin necrosis.a 2016 British Association of Plastic, Reconstructive and Aesthetic Surgeons. Summary of main results . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 533Completeness and applicability of results . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 534Agreement with similar studies . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 534Implications for future research and practice . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 535Preferred treatment strategy . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 537IntroductionRadiation therapy is used in approximately half of the 1.2million cases of invasive cancer diagnosed in the UnitedStates every year.1 Although the objective of radiotherapyis to destroy malignant cells, the surrounding normal tissuewithin the field of radiation can suffer both acute andchronic damage. Acute damage usually resolves aftertherapy is completed, but chronic damage can developmonths or years later and is collectively known as late ra-diation tissue injury (LRTI). Clinical sequelae of LRTIinclude soft tissue fibrosis, skin atrophy, epithelial ulcera-tion, skin necrosis, fistula formation, major vessel rupture,and impaired wound healing.2 Most commonly, LRTI occursafter radiation to the head and neck, breast, chest wall,and pelvic areas as patients with cancer in these sitesrespond to radiation and are likely to survive longer, thusincreasing the window for LRTI to become clinicallyevident. The incidence of LRTI varies by radiation dosage,patient age, and anatomical site; however, it has generallybeen shown to affect between 5 and 15% of long timecancer survivors.3e5Existing treatments for LRTI skin necrosis have largely

been unsatisfactory. Conservative treatments may includethe use topical retinoids, hydrocolloid dressings, tetra-chlorodecaoxide dressings, topical thrombocytic growthfactors,pentoxifylline,alpha-tocopherol,interferon-gamma, and/or topical superoxide dismutase.6,7 Surveil-lance swabs to identify infection also play an importantrole. Conservative measures may be sufficient for earlysuperficial injury, but their role in LRTI is limited as earlysurgical intervention is critically important.7 Surgical exci-sion is the first-line treatment, and options include primaryclosure and use of local flaps, skin grafts, and microvascularfree flaps. Because of the damaged state of the wound bedincluding increased fibrosis, poor vascularity, and poorgranulation tissue formation, there is a high rate of failurewhen reconstructive technique depends on local bloodflow. This may leave patients disfigured and at risk ofcomplications including wound infection and dehiscencedue to the poor vasculature of the tissue.8 Microvascularsurgery and replacement of the damaged tissue withdistant healthy tissue in the form of a musculocutaneous,fasciocutaneous, or free flap is a treatment route that canhelp fill the defect but also relies on anastomosing the flapto potentially damaged vessels. This may lead to furthercomplications and is therefore not currently viewed as afirst-line option for all patients.9Hyperbaric oxygen therapy (HBOT) is a commonly

described adjunctive treatment used for LRTI. The treat-ment involves breathing in 100% oxygen at pressuresgreater than 1 atmosphere absolute (ATA) in an airtightchamber. Although the mechanism of action of hyperbaricoxygen in irradiated tissue has not been clearly identified,it is postulated that the resultant increase in local oxygentension enhances angiogenesis, collagen formation, andantimicrobial defense.10 HBOT regimens vary, but they aretypically administered as follows: the chamber is pressur-ized to 2.0e2.5 ATA, treatment lasts for 60e120 min, thepatient is treated daily for 30e60 sessions, and treatment iscontinued depending on patient response.11 Although HBOThas been used for LRTI since 1975, there are very fewclinical studies in the literature that specifically addressskin necrosis.12 "Soft tissue injury" is a descriptor of LRTIthat is often used in case series and semi-quantitative re-views; however, outcome measures specifically addressingwound healing in patients with skin necrosis have not beensufficiently studied.The aim of this study is to provide a thorough analysis ofthe literature regarding the use of a HBOT regimen for thetreatment of LRTI skin necrosis, a complication secondaryto cancer treatment, to evaluate healing rates and guidesurgeons in HBOT's clinical role, and to propose methodsfor further investigation.MethodsSearch strategyThis review was conducted according to guidelines set forthin the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviewsand Meta-analysis (PRISMA).13A literature search was conducted to identify all articles

involving the use of hyperbaric oxygen to treat radiation-induced skin necrosis. PubMed, MEDLINE via OVID, EMBASE,WebofScience,andCochraneCentralRegisterofControlled Trials were searched using the headings andkeyword terms: ("Hyperbaric Oxygenation" OR hyperbaricOR hbo OR hbot) AND (Radiotherapy OR radiation OR lrti OR"Radiation Injury") AND ("Soft Tissue Injuries" OR "Ther-apy, Soft Tissue" OR skin necrosis OR necrosis OR radio-necrosis OR radiodermatitis OR "late radiation tissueinjury."). Refer to the Appendix for table of completesearch strategies. A manual search of relevant articlesfound from review citations was also performed. The finalsearch was performed in May 2016. In the event that anarticle was selected for inclusion but raw data was notpublished, considerable effort was made to contact thecorresponding author.Study selectionTwo reviewers (ZB and MM) independently evaluatedstudies, and a third reviewer (MG) resolved any discrep-ancies for inclusion. After identifying the relevant studiesthroughtitleandabstractinformation,studieswere

selected for inclusion after full text evaluation. Inclusioncriteria were participants with LRTI of skin or cutaneoustissue following cancer treatment; interventions acceptedwereHBOTregimens(100%oxygen,2.0e3.0ATA,30e120 min, 5e7 times weekly); any comparison groupusing standard wound care or surgical flaps was accepted.Outcome measures were 1) complete resolution and 2)symptomatic improvement of skin necrosis. Because of thepaucity of literature, all study design types were eligiblefor inclusion in this review. Articles from any country oforigin were acceptable but limited to those published in theEnglish language. Exclusion criteria were articles on HBOTthat were used to treat other radiation-induced injuries(e.g., bladder, prostate, jaw) that did not stratify skin as aspecific treatment area. Additionally, if multiple publica-tions were from the same group, only studies that reporteddata from nonoverlapping time periods were included.Data extraction and assessment of study qualityTwo authors (ZB and MM) independently extracted datausing a template data extraction sheet, with a third (MG)serving as a tiebreaker when consensus was not achieved.Information gathered included study characteristics andpatient demographics. Primary outcome was incidence ofcomplete wound healing with intervention and secondaryoutcome was incidence of symptomatic improvement withintervention. Studies were rated on methodological qualityaccording to the American Society of Plastic Surgery'sLevels of Evidence rating scale, and risk of bias for includedarticles was assessed using the NewcastleeOttawa qualityassessment scale.14

AnalysisA detailed narrative systematic review of the includedstudy outcome measures was undertaken. The primaryoutcome measure was incidence of complete wound heal-ing and secondary outcome measure was symptomaticimprovement of skin necrosis symptoms after HBOT. Thenumber of patients recorded at follow-up was used as thedenominator instead of the enrollment number. Because ofoverall study heterogeneity and lack of adequate controlgroups, a formal statistical meta-analysis was not per-formed; however, pooled rates of each outcome measurewere calculated with the available data from observationalstudies using Microsoft Excel (Redmond, WA).ResultsLiterature searchThe primary search resulted in 517 articles. Of those, 125duplicates were removed, leaving 392 articles. Afterscreening by title and abstract for articles that satisfy theinclusion criteria, 38 articles remained, and full text wasreviewed. Of these, eight met the inclusion criteria andwere included in this systematic review (Table 1). ThePRISMA search flow diagram is presented in Figure 1.The eight studies (1 uncontrolled observation cohort, 5case series, and 2 case reports) included 720 patients whoreceived HBOT for radiation-induced skin necrosis.15e22Sites of skin necrosis included lower extremity, buttocks,scrotum, abdomen, chest wall, upper extremity, and headand neck. Four of the studies provided the radiation dosedetails that caused skin necrosis; these ranged from 3000 to8500 cGy (see Table 2).

Risk of biasDespite an extensive search, no randomized controlledtrials (RCTs) were found. Because of the nature of the studydesigns, the majority of studies were at high risk for bias.All studies but one were at high risk for observer-expectancybias.15Fourstudiesprovidedadequateoutcome data to be at low risk for attrition bias.17e19,21Four studies were at high risk for procedure bias.1518The overall NewcastleeOttawa quality assessment scalescores are provided in Table 1.All the 709 patients included in this study received HBOT.The HBOT regimens were at 2.0e2.4 ATA for 90 min, 5 daysper week, with a range of 7e95 sessions and median of 32sessions per patient (Table 2). Standard wound care wascontinued in all the studies and included wet-to-drydressing changes, sharp debridement, antibiotics, and boricacid powder. Once a viable wound base was established,patients either received no further treatment and wereallowed to heal by secondary intention or underwentdelayed primary closure using various surgical techniques(split-thickness skin grafts, full-thickness skin grafts, localadvancement flaps, or artery-based flaps).ReferencesStudy typeMalignancy sitenLevel of evidenceaNOSbNiezgoda et al. (2013)Case reportChest wall1V4H&N Z Head and neck.a Assigned using American Society of Plastic Surgery's Levels of Evidence rating scale.b Assigned using NewcastleeOttawa quality assessment scale.Outcome assessmentsThis review included a total of 720 patients with radiation-induced skin necrosis. Six studies provided the primaryoutcome measure of interest, i.e., complete wound healingafter HBOT (98 patients).16e20,22 Pooled together, the rateof complete wound healing was 79.6% (78/98) (Figure 2).Two studies provided the secondary outcome measuresof interest, i.e., symptomatic improvement of skin necrosisafter HBOT (622 patients).15,21 Pooled together, the rate ofsymptomatic improvement was 86% (535/622). Furtheranalysis revealed that the rate of complete resolution ofpain symptoms was 30% (187/622), wound healing symp-toms was 30% (187/622), and wound drainage symptomswas 39% (243/622).DiscussionSummary of main resultsHBOT appears to increase the capacity of radiation-inducedskin necrosis to achieve healing and decrease associatedfrom included studies that reported wound healing outcomes.Columns indicate if HBOT was used alone, as an adjunct tosurgery, or unknown if not specifically stated in the study.LRTI Z late radiation tissue injury; HBOT Z hyperbaric oxygentherapy.ReferencesHBOT interventionStudy strengthsStudy weaknessesClinical outcomemeasuresNiezgoda2.3 ATA, 90 min, median 30sessions (range 1e90,IQR 18), mean 2.1 monthsPatients specifically stratifiedto dermal soft tissueradionecrosis, large samplesize (n Z 608)No control group,lack of statisticalsignificance,incompletepatient data

(2004)2.4 ATA, 100 min, mean 33.1sessions (range 15e60),5 per weekObjective measure ofsymptoms (by questionnaire),applied RTOG scoreRetrospectivequestionnaire recallbias potential,no control groupImproved symptomseverity2.36 ATA, 90 min, mean 42sessions (range 19e60),5 per weekImplemented symptomscoring systemKorpinar2.0 ATA, 60 min, 57 sessions,5 days per weekObjective LENT-SOMA scoreSmall n (n Z 1)Improved LENT-SOMA,transcutaneousoxygen, improvedtotal wound healingUzun2.4 ATA, 120 min, 40 sessionsSmall n (n Z 1),subjective outcomemeasureImproved totalwound healingATA Z atmosphere absolute; IQR Z interquartile range; LENT-SOMA Z late effects of normal tissue-subjective, objective, management,analytic; RTOG Z Radiation Therapy Oncology Group.symptoms. Although studies detailing the therapeutic valueof HBOT are limited in number and quality, the majority ofavailable literature has reported positive results. This re-view highlights the outcomes of these articles, which areoften excluded from systematic reviews and meta-analysis.Although conclusions cannot be drawn from level IIIeV ev-idence, pooled together, these studies portray a sound as-sociationbetweenHBOTandradiation-inducedskinnecrosis wound healing.

Completeness and applicability of resultsSix studies reported complete wound healing as the primaryoutcome measure.16e20,22 Feldmeier et al. described 23patients who had radiation-induced chest wall necrosis. Ofthose patients, six had only soft tissue necrosis, and two ofthese six (33%) healed with HBOT alone. The other fourpatients required additional surgical flap coverage but alsohealed completely.16 In another article, Feldmeier et al.reported radiation injuries to the abdomen and pelvis. Inthis series, 19 patients had skin necrosis and receivedHBOT, and 15/19 (79%) showed complete wound healing.Eleven patients healed without the need for surgicalintervention.17 A third article by Feldmeier et al. described13 patients who received HBOT for nonhealing necroticwounds of extremities in previously irradiated fields. Thesuccess rate of HBOT was 11/13 (85%), with seven requiringsurgical intervention to achieve complete wound healing.18Two of the studies that reported symptomatic improve-ment as an outcome measure used two different scoringsymptoms, and neither study had a control arm.15,21 Niez-goda et al. performed a retrospective uncontrolled obser-vational cohort study that included 2358 patients withradiation-induced injuries. Of these, 608 patients had in-juries consistent with dermal soft tissue radionecrosis(STRN). The authors reported that 100% of the STRN pa-tients had pain, fistula/wound, and wound drainage symp-toms priortoHBOT. They usedthe symptomscore

1Zworse,2Znochange,3Zimproved,and4 Z resolved. After HBOT, the mean improvement score forSTRN was 3.16  0.649, and 86.7% patients had experiencedeither improved or resolved symptoms; this was a patient-reported scale.21 Bui et al. described a series of 45 pa-tients who received HBOT for radiation injuries to varioustissues. Of these, 14 patients had radiation-induced skinnecrosis. The Radiation Therapy Oncology Group (RTOG)criteria were used to measure symptom severity before andafter treatment. Of those with skin injury, 8/14 (57%)showedimprovementpost-HBOT,5/14(36%)didnotrespond to HBOT, and one patient relapsed. In addition, Buiet al. reported that HBOT-related complications werehearing difficulties 10/45 (22%), vision problems 3/45(6.7%), and epistaxis 1/45 (2.2%). Most of these problemsresolved within the duration of the study, although twopatients with accelerated cataract formation underwentcorrective surgery. This study was limited by a lack offollow-up and relying on telephone to evaluate somepatients.15

Supplemental information such as surgical interventionbefore or after HBOT was available only for 50 patients, allin the complete wound healing outcome group. Therefore,of the remaining 670 patients included in this article, it isunknown whether surgical intervention was involved in thehealing process. This knowledge is vital to define the roleof HBOT as a definitive or adjunctive treatment for LRTIskin necrosis. Moreover, the majority of the includedstudies were at high risk of procedure bias because patientsoften received other interventions in addition to HBOT.Growth factors, steroids, diet counseling, smoking coun-seling, pentoxifylline, and vitamin E were for the most partleft uncontrolled, thereby substantially increasing the riskfor procedure bias. Because this review was limited tostudies that had outcome measures for cutaneous damageand because of a relative paucity in the literature on thissubject, we were forced to report using lower qualitypapers.Agreement with similar studiesThe majority of the HBOT literature is focused on thetreatment of radiation injuries in specific anatomic areas.Most often, the subgroups are head and neck, osteor-adionecrosis, urinary bladder, bowel, and the broadlydefined "soft tissue." In 2016 an update from an earlierCochrane systematic review and meta-analysis was per-formed on the subject of HBOT for radiation injuries. Thisreview included 14 RCTs and concluded that HBOT wasassociated with improved outcomes in LTRI of the head andneck, anus, and rectum but did not show efficacy inneurologicaltissue.TheyfoundthatHBOT increases

mucosal coverage in osteoradionecrosis of the jaw (riskratio 1.3; p Z 0.003) compared with controls. They alsoshowed an increased risk of wound breakdown followingsurgicalinterventionwithoutHBOT(riskratio4.2;p Z 0.04). Neither the Cochrane review nor any of the RCTsincluded LRTI skin necrosis as a tissue subgroup. Thus, theywere unable to make a recommendation for skin necrosistreatment.11 Another systematic review performed byHoggan et al. included 41 articles. This review had broaderinclusioncriteriaandincludedsixRCTs,fournon-randomized comparative studies, and 30 noncomparativestudies. This review found that HBOT may offer clinicalbenefits for radiation proctitis and LTRI of the head andneck. However, they stressed that the efficacy of HBOT isnot equivalent among tissue types.23On clinicaltrials.gov, there are currently seven clinicaltrials registered that involve HBOT and LRTI; however, noneof these have outcome measures that quantify skin necrosishealing in the study protocols. Presently, no RCT has testedthe efficacy of HBOT in the treatment of LRTI skin necrosis,and no evidence-based protocol has been established.Implications for future research and practiceAlthough advances have been made in targeted radiationtherapy and the rate of severe adverse effects hasdecreased, the total number of patients who suffer fromthese adverse effects remains the same. This is due to an

increased number of patients undergoing radiation treat-ment and is related to longer survival times.24 As patientsare surviving cancer longer, they may begin to reveal thetrue incidence of LRTI. In addition, the severity of lateeffects does not correlate with acute skin reaction, and thelate effects often serve to limit dose toxicity. The thresholddose of LRTI varies but has been reported at a minimumdose of 6000e8000 cGy, which can be exceeded by standardradiation therapy.25,26 Future studies need to examine therisk factors associated with the development of LRTI andpossible ways to prevent it from occurring. Because radio-protection is not yet perfected, we will need therapies thatact to reverse LRTI.The pathogenesis of LRTI is progressive in nature.

Ionizing radiation causes direct and indirect damage to cellswithin the field. Direct damage to genomic DNA can over-come cellular repair mechanisms, accumulate with mitosis,and eventually lead to cell death. Indirect damage arisesfrom the creation of toxic peroxides and free radicals fromthe reaction of cellular and interstitial water with radia-tion.27 Fibroblasts are also affected by this damage, andtheir resulting signaling and matrix deposition dysfunctionmay impair wound healing.28,29 In the skin, these mecha-nisms result in dermal fibrosis, fragmentation of elastin fi-bers, loss of rete pegs, fibrotic changes surroundingarterioles,dehiscenceofendothelialcells,andanincreased propensity for small vessels to form micro-thombi.30 Over time, these changes can lead to the dete-rioration of the soft tissue by reduction in vascularity andreplacement of healthy tissues with dense fibrotic tissue. Itis believed that the driver of this process is a lack of oxygendelivery due to a decrease in the number and quality ofblood vessels supplying the field. Eventually, a critical pointis reached when the tissue cannot maintain any amount ofnormal function, leading to break down and ulcer forma-tion, often termed "radionecrosis," which can causeextreme local pain. This process can be exacerbated oreven uncovered by surgical intervention because of addi-tional interruptions to the blood supply and decreasedability to form granulation tissue.3

Despite a lack of high-quality evidence, HBOT has beenused for radiation injuries since 1975.12 The treatment takesadvantage of Henry's law, which states the amount of gasdissolved in a liquid is equal to the partial pressure of the gasexerted on the surface of the liquid. Thus, by increasing thepartial pressure of oxygen in the chamber, significantly moreoxygen can be dissolved in blood (without the aid of hemo-globin), and because it is in the form of a solution, it canreach damaged areas that a red blood cell may not be ableto. Although the exact mechanism of action has not beenclearly identified, HBOT most likely aids in the process ofhealing through multiple mechanisms. The steep oxygengradient from healthy to irradiated tissue and increasedinspired oxygen can be a powerful inducer of angiogenesis.31HBOT is the only treatment that was proven to increase thenumber of blood vessels in radiation-damaged tissue. Marxet al. showed increased vascular density in irradiated rab-bits treated with HBOT compared to controls.10 Increasedoxygen delivery can also enhance leukocyte function andallow for a boost in wound-healing potential and possiblerisk reduction in bacterial infection.32 Study models haveDomainPurpose/notesStudy designMulticenter RCTClear randomization processand double blindingPopulationLRTI skin necrosisPtsClear allocation concealmentand blinding. Necrosis severityRTOG scoreLRTI morbidityLENT-SOMA scaleComplete healingUse dimensions toprovide % healedComplete HealingDocument time to completehealing and number ofHBOT sessionsHBOT complicationsDocument all and follow-upfor resolutionQuality of lifeUse validated assessmenttool (SF-12 or other)EvaluationPre-HBOTDocument baselinemeasures (blinded)Intra-HBOTDocument any change inmeasures (blinded)devery20th sessionPost HBOTDocument final measures(blinded)dimmediate andat 6 monthsRCT Z randomized controlled trial; LRTI Z late radiation tissueinjury; SF-12 Z short form health survey 12; RTOG Z RadiationTherapy Oncology Group.HBOT Z hyperbaric oxygen therapy.shown that HBOT can reverse the effects of LRTI by inducingfibroblast proliferation and angiogenesis in a variety of tis-sues;theexceptionbeingneurologicaltissue,whichremainedresistant.10,33Feldmeieretal.displayeddecreased fibrosis in the intestinal wall of irradiated micetreated with HBOT compared to controls.34We have summarized all articles relevant to this topic,with inclusion criteria being necessarily broad to capture allpossible articles. With the association between HBOT andLRTI skin necrosis being highlighted, we hope to emphasizethat evidence for this subject is minimal and much explo-ration needs to be undertaken in the future. Further clin-ical trials with larger recruitment and carefully plannedintervention and outcome measures are needed in thefuture. A well-planned trial as highlighted in Table 3 wouldbe the first of its kind and serve to answer many questionsabout HBOT and LRTI skin necrosis.Preferred treatment strategy

The senior author has been treating LRTI skin necrosis withand without HBOT for over 10 years. Although not evidencedbased, we have developed a treatment strategy that en-compasses the indications for HBOT and a protocol to guidetherapy. Indications to begin HBOT largely depend on fac-tors such as the size of the LRTI, the availability and consentfor a free flap, and the presence of an implant (Figure 3).Once LRTI skin necrosis is diagnosed and reaches a signifi-cant size, the clinician should begin to consider the recon-structive options. If the patient is amenable to undergoingfree flap surgery and the surgeon has this technique avail-able as an option, then the damaged tissue should beexcised and a healthy recipient vessel should be identifiedto be anastomosed to an autologous free tissue flap.Because the area in question will be excised in its entirety,there is usually little role for HBOT here. If for some reasonfree flaps are not an option, then the presence of an implantand its risk for exposure and infection is a factor that shouldbe considered. If an implant is not present, then we proceedwith standard local wound care for 1 month. If there is noimprovement, then we avail the option to begin adjunctiveHBOT for these patients as it is covered by Medicare andmost other insurances. However, if an implant is present andis considered to be at risk, we recommend to begin theHBOT protocol immediately. The protocol consists of 30HBOT sessions with periodic minor debridements, followedby a reassessment of the wound. If significant progress ismade and there is a chance for healing by delayed primaryintention, then HBOT is continued until wound closure. Ifafter 30 sessions, the wound bed appears healthy but willrequire local tissue rearrangement to obtain soft tissuecoverage, then surgery is planned, performed, and followedby 10 HBOT sessions. Finally, if after 30 sessions the woundlooks the same or worse, then the patient should beeducated on the necessity of a free flap.

ConclusionHBOT has been used to treat LRTI skin necrosis despiteinsufficient evidence for its application. Although it hasbeen proven to be a safe intervention, its efficacy fortreating different types of damaged tissue is not consistent.We have reported a general trend of HBOT success for LRTIskin necrosis, but our review is limited by the quality ofstudies included. A well-designed randomized clinical trialthat measures the efficacy of HBOT alone in treating LRTIskin necrosis is needed to validate this intervention anddevelop an evidence-based protocol.Funding disclosureAcknowledgmentsNone.Appendix A.
